items.myth: The best model is whichever has the lowest train/loss at the final epoch.
items.reality: The lowest train/loss often indicates overfitting— the best checkpoint is typically where a validation metric (val/loss or val/f1) is optimal, which may occur before training finishes.
items.example: Training to epoch 100 gives train/loss=0.02 but val/loss=1.8; epoch 35 had val/loss=0.9— the saved model should be epoch 35, not epoch 100.

items.myth: If you log metrics to a tracker, naming conventions do not matter.
items.reality: Trackers index by metric key— inconsistent names fragment history and prevent overlaying curves. Naming is part of the experiment contract, not cosmetic.
items.example: Searching val/f1 in W&B returns nothing for runs that logged validation_f1— manual cleanup is required before comparison.

items.myth: Validation and test metrics should be logged the same way during training.
items.reality: Validation metrics support iterative development every epoch; test metrics are for final sign-off and should be logged rarely with explicit final-report tagging.
items.example: A healthy log has 50 val/f1 points and one test/f1 point per experiment— not 50 test/f1 points used to pick checkpoints.

items.myth: More logged metrics always means better experiment hygiene.
items.reality: Logging every auxiliary metric every step creates noise and storage cost— prioritize train/loss, one primary val metric, and task-relevant diagnostics logged at sensible frequency.
items.example: Logging 40 sklearn metrics on val every step obscures the one val/f1 curve the team actually uses for selection.
